skill.name: ml-training-debugger
skill.description: Diagnose machine learning training failures by reviewing logs, loss curves, model code, gradients, hyperparameters, and architecture signals.
skill.summary: Find likely root causes for ML training failures and suggest evidence-based fixes.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are false positives caused by markdown code fences, inline backticks, and ML terms. The confirmed concern is that the documented agent configuration allows the Bash tool, which can execute local commands if implemented, so this skill should publish with an external-command warning.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Capable Tool Enabled for Spawned Agent
medium_findings.description: The SDK example configures allowed_tools with Bash for the specialist agent. The surrounding text describes read-only analysis and no malicious command is present, but Bash access can run local commands if this design is implemented without strict permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Bash tool is explicitly listed in the agent options, which confirms external command capability. Confidence is below very high because this is documented sample logic, not a complete executable implementation in the scanned files.

medium_findings.title: Untrusted Training Artifacts Passed Into Agent Task
medium_findings.description: The skill builds a specialist-agent task from user-provided symptoms and artifact paths. This is core functionality, but training logs or model files can contain misleading instructions, so command-capable analysis should treat project artifacts as untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context flow is explicit, but no prompt injection text was found in the skill files themselves. The concern is contextual because the agent may analyze untrusted user artifacts while Bash is available.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings occur in markdown JSON examples, inline file references, and documentation text. I found no Ruby backtick execution expression or shell command string in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited regions are visible markdown fences, JSON examples, inline resource names, and Python sample code. They do not contain Ruby backtick execution semantics.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptographic-algorithm locations do not contain hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or cryptographic APIs. They are normal skill description, ML debugging process, or example output text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of each cited line found no cryptographic operation. The pattern appears to be triggered by unrelated text in markdown and Python documentation.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Findings Are Domain Diagnostics
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance findings point to ML debugging concepts such as CUDA errors, loss-curve extraction, and ACT loss diagnostics. I found no host enumeration, network scanning, or external endpoint probing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plainly about ML training behavior and framework errors. No commands, host discovery logic, or network targets are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash Tool Exposure
dangerous_patterns.description: The agent options include Bash in allowed_tools. This is useful for diagnostics but should be constrained to read-only inspection commands and reviewed before marketplace publication.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous capability is explicitly present as a configured tool. No malicious use is shown, so the pattern is a capability risk rather than confirmed abuse.

content.value_statement: Machine learning training failures are hard to debug when logs, gradients, and model code point in different directions. This skill organizes the evidence, identifies likely root causes, and recommends fixes with verification steps.
actual_capabilities: Classifies symptoms such as loss divergence, mode collapse, vanishing gradients, and GPU memory failures.
Reviews available training logs, loss curves, model code, error messages, and configuration files.
Ranks likely root causes using evidence from artifacts and reported training behavior.
Suggests prioritized fixes with expected impact, effort, risk, and verification steps.
Requests missing artifacts when confidence is too low for a useful diagnosis.
limitations: It does not train models or validate fixes by running full experiments.
It depends on the quality and completeness of the provided logs and code.
It may need human review for rare model architectures or contradictory evidence.
The documented Bash capability requires careful permission controls in real use.

use_cases.title: Investigate Loss Divergence
use_cases.description: Analyze the point where loss increases, compare gradients and learning rates, and choose safe first fixes.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Degenerate Model Outputs
use_cases.description: Review mode collapse symptoms, output diversity, architecture balance, and training history to find likely causes.

use_cases.title: Triage Training Infrastructure Errors
use_cases.description: Use error traces, checkpoint details, device placement, and memory signals to separate code issues from system issues.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Training Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: My model stopped learning. Review the symptoms I provide and tell me which logs, config files, and code files you need first.

prompt_templates.title: Loss Divergence Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate debugging with logs and curves
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose this loss divergence using my training logs, loss curve data, optimizer settings, and model code. Rank likely causes and fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Mode Collapse Investigation
prompt_templates.prompt: My model returns nearly identical outputs for different inputs. Analyze architecture balance, output diversity, loss behavior, and training configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Full Root Cause Audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a complete ML training failure audit. Use logs, gradients, architecture code, checkpoints, and configuration to produce evidence-ranked root causes.

output_examples.input: Loss falls for six epochs, then rises sharply after a curriculum transition.
output: Primary diagnosis: learning rate or transition schedule instability is likely.
Evidence to check: gradient norm spike, optimizer group rates, and data mix changes at the transition.
Recommended fix: reduce the affected learning rate, enable clipping, and rerun a short validation window.

output_examples.input: The model answers every prompt with the same short token sequence.
output: Primary diagnosis: mode collapse or architecture capacity imbalance is likely.
Evidence to check: output diversity, embedding parameter share, logit variance, and training loss behavior.
Recommended fix: rebalance capacity, add diversity regularization, and verify output entropy improves.

output_examples.input: Training fails with CUDA memory errors during larger batches.
output: Primary diagnosis: batch size, activation memory, or checkpointing configuration needs review.
Evidence to check: peak memory, sequence length, accumulation settings, and model component sizes.
Recommended fix: lower microbatch size, use gradient accumulation, and profile memory again.

best_practices: Provide logs, configuration, model code, and exact failure timing together.
Ask for evidence-ranked causes before applying training changes.
Verify each fix with a short controlled rerun and clear success metrics.
anti_patterns: Do not ask for broad hyperparameter advice without logs or symptoms.
Do not apply multiple major fixes at once without measuring each effect.
Do not allow command execution on untrusted projects without reviewing permissions.

faq.question: Can this skill fix my training code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is designed to diagnose failures and recommend changes for review.

faq.question: What artifacts should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide training logs, loss curves, model code, optimizer settings, configuration files, and error traces when available.

faq.question: Does it support PyTorch?
faq.answer: Yes. The prompt includes PyTorch and CUDA debugging concepts, including autograd, dataloaders, checkpoints, and memory issues.

faq.question: Can it diagnose mode collapse?
faq.answer: Yes. It reviews output diversity, logit behavior, architecture balance, and loss design signals.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The documented agent configuration allows Bash for analysis. This is useful but requires permission controls.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning about external command capability and careful review of runtime permissions.
